text: were quite similar. In fact, there are more than a few parallels between these two crucial thinkers, pushed to the margins of the social sciences in much the same way. Sociologists now read Tarde but will not be able to place van Gennep; anthropologists know a bit about van Gennep but are unable to place Tarde – another sign of the institutional split between folklore/ ethnography and sociology and the almost total lack of interdisciplinary dialogue within branches of the social sciences which were once part and parcel of the same larger field. Even scholars of Marcel Mauss, including Marcel Fournier (2006), have paid surprisingly little attention to the important overlaps between the work of Mauss and van Gennep as two complementary and highly original thinkers. The clear tendency in the literature on Mauss is to see van Gennep as an opponent to all the Durkheimians, evidently – and centrally – including Mauss. As will be discussed below, this is only partially true. Mauss scholars, for example, uncritically take Mauss’ very negative 1910 review of Rites of Passage at face value, and use it as a proof that Mauss, just like Durkheim, held van Gennep’s work in very low regard. They omit to discuss how Mauss’ post-World War I writings actually moved closer and closer to the original positions of van Gennep, and further and further away from Durkheim. This is of course not something Mauss would ever say explicitly, but it shows from the substance of his later writings: his notion of habitus, his insistence on bodily techniques, his comparative dimension which in his work on personhood re-incorporated a historical-genealogical dimension, his close and insistent attention to etymology, his re-engagement with psychology, his relational stance on social phenomena, even his belated understanding of the political peril embedded in Durkheim’s collectivist creed – all of these developments in Mauss’ oeuvre bring him in close contact with van Gennep. While recognizing that van Gennep did eventually make a shift in orientation from ethnography and sociology toward French folklore, we need to engage with Arnold van Gennep’s life’s work and its foundational value for the social sciences, before putting him into boxes, burying him in disciplinary quarrels or reducing Liminality and the Modern 30 his efforts to an embryonic beginning of whatever -ism. We need to bring the potential of his academic project to life again, in order to be able to build on to the framework he left behind. This is the aim of the first two chapters of this book. Missing Rites of Passage: The Early Life and Work of Charles-Arnold Kurr van Gennep The man we today talk about as Arnold van Gennep was born as Charles-Arnold Kurr van Gennep on April 23 in 1873 in Ludwigsburg, Würtemberg. His father was lieutenant at the court of Würtemberg, which was then an independent kingdom (another genius, Einstein, was born in nearby Ulm in 1879, the same year van Gennep left the kingdom). His mother was of Dutch origin. His father descended from French migrants. Both French and Dutch were spoken at home. The fact that he grew up in a tri-lingual environment and within a migrant family would later serve him well. At the age of 8 he was, according to Ketty van Gennep (1964: 9) fluent in reading and speaking French, German and Dutch. When he was six years old his parents separated (due to a certain ‘incompabilité d’humeur’, as his daughter would diplomatically state it, likely repeating an expression she had heard from her father; ibid.: 4), and van Gennep moved with his mother to live in Lyons, France. From what can be reconstructed, he lost all contact with his biological father. There can be little doubt that this sudden and radical separation from his father and his childhood environment left deep traces in his personality. Arnold van Gennep: A Cosmopolitan Frenchman In the various encyclopaedia entries that exist, Arnold van Gennep is variously introduced as a ‘Belgian’, ‘Dutch’ or ‘German’ anthropologist or folklorist. While van Gennep’s mother was indeed ‘Dutch’, and while he was in a formal sense born in a Germanic state, the truth is that Van Gennep was French before all else. He moved to France at the age of six, and except shorter stays abroad (including fieldwork periods in Algeria and his three year spell at Neuchâtel in Switzerland) he studied and lived in France the rest of his life. French also became his language of intimacy. This, however, would not prevent Durkheim from questioning his Frenchness when van Gennep during World War I tried, once again, to get his foot inside the French academic job market (more on which in Chapter 2). In 1884 Van Gennep’s mother married a French doctor, Paul Raugé, who had a summer practice in the province of Savoy. Van Gennep’s strong attachment to this region, which he would come to consider his adopted homeland, dates from his early youth. He loved Savoy. He was to travel through Savoy, village by village, collecting ethnographic and folkloric materials, learning the dialects, rituals and traditions of each single village. This material would make up some of the data later to be used in his monumental work on French folklore. Arnold van Gennep: Fragments of a Life-Work at the Thresholds 31 However, if van Gennep was in most ways simply French, it is also true that his background made him quite different from most other French and Parisian intellectuals, with whom he would come into contact. He did not really belong anywhere, and he was difficult to categorize – then as now. He possessed knowledge that would surprise everyone. He had read things that no other intellectual in France knew off. Van Gennep’s sublime linguistic skills meant that he more than any other person working within ethnology and anthropology in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries gathered and read literature from all corners of Europe.
